d: A formal record of Aurangabad (Khadki · Fatehnagar · now Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar) — founded 1610 by Malik Ambar, made imperial Mughal capital of the Deccan by Aurangzeb in 1653. Bibi ka Maqbara, Panchakki, Daulatabad Fort, Khuldabad, the city of fifty-two gates, and the first capital of the Asaf Jahi state.

A formal record of Bidar — capital of the Bahmani Sultanate from 1429 and seat of the Barid Shahis. The Bidar Fort, Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, the Bahmani Tombs at Ashtur, the Barid Shahi necropolis, the Rangin Mahal and the lineage of Bidriware.

A formal record of Bijapur (Vijayapura) — capital of the Adil Shahi sultanate from 1490 to 1686. The Gol Gumbaz, Ibrahim Rauza, Jami Masjid, Malik-e-Maidan, the Adil Shahi sultans and the long siege of Aurangzeb.

A formal record of Golconda — the granite-hill fortress that was the Qutb Shahi capital from 1518 to 1591, the source of the world's most legendary diamonds (Koh-i-Noor, Hope, Regent, Orlov), the eight-month siege of Aurangzeb in 1687, and the seven-tomb necropolis of its sultans.

A formal record of Gulbarga (Kalaburagi) — the original Bahmani capital from 1347. The Gulbarga Fort, the unique closed-court Jama Masjid of 1367, the Haft Gumbaz necropolis, and the great Sufi shrine of Khwaja Banda Nawaz Gesudaraz.

A formal record of Hyderabad — founded 1591 by Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah, capital of the Qutb Shahi Sultanate and the Asaf Jahi Nizams. Charminar, Golconda, Mecca Masjid, the Qutb Shahi Tombs, Falaknuma and Chowmahalla, the long durbar of the Nizams and the events of September 1948.
The cities of the Dakhni heartland — Hyderabad, Bidar, Gulbarga, Bijapur, Aurangabad, Golconda, Warangal, Nanded, Raichur and Nizamabad. Six centuries of Deccan civilisation, in ten cities.

A formal record of Nanded — the ancient Nandigrama on the Godavari, a Bahmani and Nizam Shahi outpost, the Mughal frontier town where Guru Gobind Singh spent his last days, and the seat of Takht Sachkhand Sri Hazur Abchalnagar Sahib — one of the five Takhts of Sikhism.

A formal record of Nizamabad — the ancient city of Indur on the Godavari tributary, fortified by the Bahmanis and renamed under the Asaf Jahis; home of the celebrated black lacquerware tradition and the Nizamsagar Dam.

A formal record of Raichur — the Kakatiya fortress on the Tungabhadra–Krishna doab, the most contested ground between the Bahmanis, Vijayanagara and Bijapur, site of Krishnadevaraya's 1520 victory, and a Hyderabad State district under the Asaf Jahis until 1948.

A formal record of Warangal — the city of one stone, capital of the Kakatiya dynasty (1163–1323). Warangal Fort and its four toranas, the Thousand Pillar Temple at Hanamkonda, the Ramappa Temple (UNESCO 2021), Rudrama Devi and Prataparudra II, and Warangal under the Bahmanis, Qutb Shahis and Asaf Jahi Nizams.

The Bahmani Sultanate (1347–1527): the first independent Muslim kingdom of the Deccan, founded by Alauddin Bahman Shah at Gulbarga and later Bidar, whose court under Mahmud Gawan laid the foundation of Dakhni civilisation.

The Bahmani Sultanate (1347–1527): the first independent Muslim kingdom of the Deccan, founded by Alauddin Bahman Shah at Gulbarga and later Bidar, whose court under Mahmud Gawan laid the foundation of Dakhni civilisation.

The Bahmani Sultanate (1347–1527): the first independent Muslim kingdom of the Deccan, founded by Alauddin Bahman Shah at Gulbarga and later Bidar, whose court under Mahmud Gawan laid the foundation of Dakhni civilisation.

The Barid Shahi Sultanate of Bidar (1489–1619): successors to the Bahmanis, patrons of Bidriware metal-craft and Sufi scholarship, whose lobed tombs are among the finest examples of Deccan funerary architecture.
Six centuries of Deccan rule: the Bahmani Sultanate, Qutb Shahi, Bidar Barid Shahi, Adil Shahi and Asaf Jahi Nizams — from Gulbarga to Hyderabad, 1347 to 1948.

The Qutb Shahi Dynasty (1518–1687): rulers of Golconda and founders of Hyderabad in 1591, greatest patrons of Dakhni literature and masters of the diamond trade that made the kingdom legendary from Madrid to Edo.
Architecture of the Deccan — Bahmani fortresses, Qutb Shahi minarets, Adil Shahi domes.
Bidriware, Paithani silk, Hyderabadi pearls and Deccan miniatures — the material culture of the Deccan.
Hyderabadi cuisine — a kitchen at the crossroads of empires. Biryani, haleem, marag, lukhmi and khubani ka meetha.
A year in the Dakhni calendar — Ramazan at Charminar, Muharram marsiya, Bonalu, the Deccan Festival.

The living heritage of the Deccan — Dakhni dialect and poetry, Hyderabadi cuisine, Hindustani music in the southern courts, Bidriware and Paithan crafts, Sufi orders and the festivals that still mark a Dakhni year.
Dakhni — the southern dialect of Urdu; a tongue forged in the bazaar and refined at the court.
Music in the Deccan — the nine ragas, the Hyderabadi gharana, qawwali at the dargah.
The Sufi tradition of the Deccan — Chishti, Qadiri and Suhrawardi orders, dargahs that have drawn pilgrims for six centuries.

Dakhni.org is the digital home of Deccan heritage — six centuries of Bahmani, Qutb Shahi, Adil Shahi, Barid Shahi and Asaf Jahi history, Dakhni (a dialect of Urdu born in the Deccan), Hyderabadi cuisine, Sufi poetry and the living culture of the Deccan plateau.
The landmarks of the Deccan — the monuments, forts, palaces and tombs of the plateau, and the great institutions of learning, medicine and culture founded across the Deccan and Hyderabad State.
Founded in 1922 by the Anjuman-e-Taraqqi-e-Urdu, Anwar-ul-Uloom College on Mallepally became a principal community-led seat of Urdu scholarship in Hyderabad.
Founded in 1865 as the Madrasa-i-Aliya and later renamed City College — among the oldest schools of Hyderabad, with its long Indo-Saracenic frontage on the Musi.
Founded in 1929 by the seventh Nizam as the Hyderabad State College of Engineering, now JNTU Hyderabad — a principal engineering institution of the state.
Founded in 1923 as the Jagirdars' College to educate the children of the Hyderabad State nobility, renamed Hyderabad Public School in 1951.

Historic institutions of the Deccan — Osmania University, Jamia Nizamia, Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, Nizam College, Tibbi College and the great schools, colleges and academies of the Bahmani, Qutb Shahi and Asaf Jahi eras.
Founded in 1876 by Mawlana Anwarullah Farooqui (Fazilat-Jung), Jamia Nizamia is the principal Sunni Hanafi seminary of the Deccan.
Founded in 1879 at Secunderabad by Sir Salar Jung I, Mahbub College was among the earliest Western-style institutions of the Hyderabad State.
Founded in 1907 by the sixth Nizam Mir Mahbub Ali Khan, Mahbubia was among the earliest girls' schools in South India.
The three-storey Persian-style madrasa raised in 1472 at Bidar by the Bahmani wazir Khwaja Mahmud Gawan — astronomers, jurists and a library of three thousand manuscripts.
Founded in 1887 under the sixth Nizam Mir Mahbub Ali Khan, Nizam College was the first modern liberal-arts college of the Hyderabad State.
Founded in 1894 under Asaf Jahi patronage, the Nizamia Tibbi College gave the Unani medical tradition of the Deccan a modern institutional form.
Built in 1925 by the seventh Nizam Mir Osman Ali Khan, the Indo-Saracenic block of Osmania General Hospital stands on the Musi as part of his post-1908 public-health programme.
Founded in 1846 as the Hyderabad Medical School and renamed Osmania Medical College in 1919, it is among the oldest medical colleges in India.
Founded in 1918 by the seventh Nizam Mir Osman Ali Khan, Osmania was the first university in modern India to teach in an Indian language — Urdu.
Opened in 1951, the Salar Jung Museum on the Musi is the personal collection of Mir Yousuf Ali Khan, Salar Jung III — said to be the third-largest one-man art collection in the world.
Founded in 1891 as the Asafia State Library, the State Central Library at Afzal Gunj holds one of the largest collections of Urdu, Persian and Arabic manuscripts in South Asia.

Architectural marvels of the Deccan — Charminar, Golconda Fort, Gol Gumbaz, the Qutb Shahi tombs, Daulatabad, Bidar fort, Chowmahalla and Falaknuma palaces, and the great forts, palaces and tomb-gardens of the Bahmani, Qutb Shahi, Adil Shahi and Asaf Jahi dynasties.
Dakhni — a literature older than its descendants. The southern register of Urdu that took shape in the Deccan between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries, and still survives as everyday speech.
Faarsi (Persian) in the Deccan — from Mahmud Gawan's correspondence with Iran to the last Persian firman of the Nizams in 1885. The language of court, chancery and treaty.
The four tongues of the Deccan — Dakhni, Urdu, Faarsi (Persian) and Telugu — a formal record of seven centuries of plateau letters.
Telugu — the spoken substrate of the Deccan plateau, present in every market, every Hindu shrine, every village beneath the sultanates' Persian courts.
Urdu in the Deccan — from the soldier's tongue of the Tughluq camp to the printed page of the nineteenth century. The longest thread in the cultural history of the plateau.

The great Sufi shrines of the Deccan — Khuldabad, Bandanawaz at Gulbarga, Yousufain Sharif and Maula Ali at Hyderabad, Pahadi Sharif, Shah Khamosh and the dargahs of the Bahmani, Qutb Shahi and Asaf Jahi periods.
The sacred sites of the Deccan — the historic masjids, the Sufi dargahs, the ancient temples and the shrines of other faiths that together make up the religious landscape of the plateau.

The historic mosques of the Deccan — Mecca Masjid, Toli Masjid, Jama Masjid Golconda, the Adil Shahi mosques of Bijapur, the Bahmani Jama at Gulbarga, the Solah Khamba and Jama Masjid of Bidar, and the great Asaf Jahi mosques of Hyderabad.

t: Other Sacred Sites of the Deccan
d: Beside its masjids, dargahs and temples, the Deccan plateau has long held smaller but no less important communities of Sikhs, Christians, Parsis, Jains and (more recently) Bahá'ís. From the gurdwara at Nanded, where the tenth Sikh Guru lived his final years, to the basalt cathedral at Medak — one of

The historic temples of the Deccan plateau — Ramappa and Thousand Pillar of the Kakatiyas, Bhadrachalam, Yadagirigutta, Vemulawada, the Ellora caves and Grishneshwar, Birla Mandir Hyderabad and the great pilgrimage temples of Telangana, Andhra and the wider Deccan.
Khwaja Sayyid Muhammad Husayni Gesudaraz Bandanawaz (1321–1422) — the most learned Sufi of the medieval Deccan, central figure of Chishti Islam in southern India.
A formal record of Khwaja Burhanuddin Gharib (d. 1337) — the first Chishti shaykh to settle in the Deccan, founder of the Khuldabad hospice and the Deccan Chishti tradition.
Hazrat Hussain Shah Wali (d. 1599) — the Qutb Shahi scholar who surveyed and built the Hussain Sagar reservoir at Hyderabad.

A formal record of Sufism (Tasawwuf) in the Deccan — the science of Ihsan within traditional Sunni Islam, the four classical silsilas, and the lives of the saints who shaped the spiritual life of the plateau from Khuldabad and Gulbarga to Hyderabad.
Hazrat Sayyid Shah Khamosh (d. 1740) — the silent saint of the early Asaf Jahi period at Hyderabad, master of the Naqshbandi-Qadiri silsila.
Hazrat Sayyid Shah Raju Qattal Qadiri (d. c. 1632) — founder of the Qadiri silsila at Hyderabad and ancestor of the Yousufain and Mahbub Subhani lineages.
Hazrat Sayyid Shah Muhammad Sharfuddin Suhrawardi · Pahadi Sharif, Hyderabad. The most-loved Sufi shrine of the city.
Sayyid Shah Yousuf-uddin and Sayyid Shah Sharif-uddin Husayni — the two Qadiri brothers whose joint shrine at Nampally is the spiritual centre of modern Hyderabad.
